nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats, lagoons, and reef slopes; typically attached to rocky substrate or coral rubble in moderate to strong water movement

decorPreferences: open placement with spacing from neighboring corals
ample horizontal space for cap expansion

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental broadcast feeding of micro-plankton, phytoplankton, or foods designed for filter-feeding invertebrates 1–2 times weekly is optional but may support growth — the coral's nutritional needs are generally met by adequate lighting.

avoidWith: SPS corals (Acropora and similar) in confined volumes — terpenoid allelopathy
Catalaphyllia, Plerogyra, Euphyllia spp. — documented chemical sensitivity
other leather corals placed in direct contact
coral-nipping fish (large angelfish, butterflyfish, large crabs)

summary: Sarcophyton is a genus of iconic large soft corals popular with beginners for their hardiness and striking toadstool shape. Moderate light and flow; allelopathic terpenoid chemistry requires spacing from SPS corals and activated carbon filtration.
careNotes:
Note: Sarcophyton is a genus of over 50 described species, commonly traded without species identification. Species commonly encountered in the reef aquarium trade include S. ehrenbergi, S. glaucum, and S. trocheliophorum. All share near-identical husbandry requirements, so this entry is written at the genus level.

Sarcophyton corals are considered among the most beginner-friendly soft corals available. They tend to tolerate a wider range of water parameters than SPS or demanding LPS corals and generally recover well from minor fluctuations.

Key care considerations:

• Lighting: Generally advised to keep in the 75–200 PAR range. Newly introduced specimens should be placed lower in the aquascape and gradually acclimated upward over 1–2 weeks to avoid photoshock. They have been documented tolerating a wide range of intensities, but tend to perform best in the 100–150 PAR sweet spot.

• Flow: Moderate indirect flow is preferred. Enough current to gently sway polyps promotes gas exchange and assists in mucus shedding. Direct, narrow powerhead blasts have been known to cause retraction and tissue stress.

• Placement: Middle rockwork placement is generally recommended, with ample horizontal space for the cap to expand. Colonies commonly reach 30–60 cm in diameter under aquarium conditions; wild specimens can exceed 90 cm. Plan spacing accordingly.

• Allelopathy — IMPORTANT: Sarcophyton is among the more potent chemical competitors in the reef hobby. It manufactures terpenoid compounds (including sarcophytoxide) that suppress photosynthesis in neighboring corals, especially SPS corals like Acropora. Chemical influence has been reported extending 15–20 cm from the colony. In tanks dominated by sensitive SPS corals, Sarcophyton is generally considered incompatible without robust activated carbon filtration, a high-quality protein skimmer, and regular water changes. Running activated carbon continuously is widely considered essential when keeping Sarcophyton alongside SPS species.

• Mucus shedding: Sarcophyton periodically retracts its polyps and develops a waxy, semi-transparent mucus coating on its cap surface before shedding it. This cycle can last from a few days to 2–3 weeks and tends to alarm new keepers. It is a normal self-cleaning behavior — the coral is shedding algae, detritus, and accumulated surface debris. Increased flow during this period helps the coral release the waxy layer faster. The shed mucus can cloud aquarium water temporarily; running activated carbon or performing a water change after shedding is advisable.

• Supplements: Some sources note benefits from regular trace element supplementation (iodine, strontium). Unlike SPS or LPS corals, Sarcophyton has no calcium carbonate skeleton and does not consume calcium or magnesium at meaningful rates; maintaining standard reef-water values is sufficient.

• Tank age: Generally considered suitable for established reef systems (3+ months), though it tolerates newer tanks more readily than SPS corals.

• Fragging: Among the easiest corals to propagate. Cap sections or stalk cuts readily regenerate into new colonies when placed on frag plugs or rubble. Cut frags may temporarily retract and shed before opening; this is normal and should not be mistaken for tissue necrosis.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard propagation method in captivity and is widely practiced. Sharp cuts from the cap or stalk readily develop into new colonies on frag plugs within 1–2 weeks of healing. In the wild, Sarcophyton reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning, with males (typically smaller, ~11 cm) releasing sperm clouds and females (reaching 60+ cm) releasing egg bundles. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Toadstool Mushroom Leather Coral (Brown)
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12), care level (Easy), compatibility, and feeding notes. Calcium and magnesium not specified; cross-checked against secondaries.

additional.name: ReefBay – Complete Toadstool Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 75–80°F / 24–27°C, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–11, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm), lighting (75–250 PAR, optimal 100–150), minimum tank size (30 gal minimum / 50 gal preferred), adult colony size (30+ cm cap), and mucus shedding cycle (1–3 weeks). PAR range and temperature range adopted from this source averaged with others.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Care Requirements of Toadstool Leather in Home Reef Aquariums
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temperature 76–80°F / 24–27°C, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–10, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm), lighting (100–200 PAR), flow (moderate to strong indirect), placement (middle to upper rockwork), and compatibility (avoid angelfish, butterflyfish, large crabs). Calcium and magnesium values confirmed consistent with ReefBay.

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping – Toadstool Leather Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (25–27°C), salinity (1.025 SG), alkalinity (7.5–9.0 dKH), PAR (80–250 sweet spot 100–150), flow (moderate indirect), and adult colony size (20+ cm cap). Noted minimal calcium/magnesium demand for this soft coral.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Sarcophyton Leather Corals: Attractive, Toxic, and Tough as Nails
additional.notes: Secondary verification of allelopathic behavior (among the more noxious chemical competitors in reef tanks), polyp retraction / shedding cycle (documented as normal tissue cleansing), adult wild colony size (some species exceed 90 cm / 3 ft diameter), and propagation by fragging. Taxonomy confirmed via Wikipedia (Order: Malacalcyonacea, Family: Alcyoniidae).
